titleStmt.title: Letter from Philip C. MacDermot to Henry Arthur Wynne, 27 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Philip C. MacDermot

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Philip MacDermot (b. 1863), the crown solicitor for Roscommon to Sir Henry Arthur Wynne (1867 – 1945). This letter concerns MacDermot’s employment of a typewriter in order to complete the number of warrants to be issued under the “Crimes Act”. MacDermot is looking to be reimbursed for the salary he was required to pay this typewriter which ,as he outlines, was essential in order to complete his work.Henry Arthur Wynne (1867-1945) was the chief crown solicitor for Ireland following the death of Malchy Kelly (1950-1916).

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Office of Crown Solicitor for Co. Roscommon, Boyle, 27th July, 1916. Dear Sir, With reference to the cases under the 'Crimes Act'
about which I had an interview with you on Tuesday, I found
it absolutely necessary yesterday at Roscommon to employ a
typewriter to type the warrants which, you are aware, are
very long as you advised me to set out the full charge as
drafted by you in the Summonses, and so as to get the matter
completed yesterday (and this was not done until eight
o'clock p.m.) I had, with the District Inspector, to find
a Typewriter and agree to pay him 11/6. so will you kindly
let me know if you can arrange for me to get a refund of
this? As you were aware the matter was urgent, and if all
these warrants had to be written out, or if I was not able
to get assistance at Roscommon yesterday, they could not
have been completed in time, as it was, the Resident
Magistrate had to wait until eight o'clock. The Police
witnesses, who had to make the informations, were unable to
arrive at Roscommon until the one o'clock train. Yours truly, Philip C.J McDermot.
The Chief Crown Solicitor, Dublin Castle, Attorney General/
In the circumstances I think it would be reasonable and
fair to allow Mr. MacDermot the 11/6 paid typewriter.
H.A.W. ,
29.7.1916 Let the sum asked for be paid.
J.H.C. ,
1.8.'16 Mr Taylor/
H.A.WYNNE,
2.7.'16
